ontology.foodex2_description: Potato flour — processed tuber-derived flour product; FoodEx2 category for starchy root and tuber flours
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated potato-flour leaf code; A000E (starchy roots and tubers, processed) is the closest defensible parent node. Exact leaf code not available in public FoodEx2 browser as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for potato flour confirmed in public browser; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potato flour used as a thickener or binder is approximately 1 tablespoon (12 g), providing around 43 kcal and 10 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Potato flour — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05040009 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 20094, Potato flour) is used as a secondary cross-reference for saturated fat (0.09 g/100g) and is documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 85 for potato flour is consistent with published values for dehydrated/processed potato products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Potato flour is a concentrated starch product derived from whole cooked potato; GI of 85 (high) is a defensible estimate anchored to the closest published potato-product GI values (instant mashed potato GI ~85, baked potato GI ~85). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × net carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (85 × 9.26) / 100 = 7.87 ≈ 7.9 for a 12 g serving (net carbohydrate = (83.1 − 5.9) × 0.12 = 9.26 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1,001 mg/100g) with very low sodium (55 mg/100g) yields an outstanding Na:K ratio strongly aligned with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Potato flour is suitable for individuals with hypertension when used in small quantities as a thickener or binder; its very high potassium content actively supports blood pressure regulation, but the high carbohydrate load means portion control is essential to avoid weight gain that could indirectly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high carbohydrate content (83.1 g/100g) combined with a high glycemic index (GI 85) produces a significant postprandial glucose spike even with 5.9 g/100g dietary fibre partially moderating absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should use potato flour only in very small quantities as a thickener or binder (≤1 tablespoon per dish) and should consider substituting with lower-GI alternatives such as almond flour or coconut flour for baking applications to minimise postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.34 g/100g), negligible saturated fat (0.09 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (1,001 mg/100g), and meaningful dietary fibre (5.9 g/100g) collectively support a favourable cardiovascular nutrient profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Potato flour poses low direct cardiovascular risk due to its negligible fat and cholesterol content and high potassium; however, its very high glycemic load means it should be used sparingly in cardiovascular disease management to avoid contributing to hyperglycaemia, insulin resistance, and associated cardiometabolic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade-derived solanine content and very high glycemic load (promoting pro-inflammatory mediator production) make potato flour potentially problematic for RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid or strictly limit potato flour and consider non-nightshade starch alternatives such as rice flour, tapioca starch, or arrowroot powder.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato flour is a fine, smooth, off-white to cream-coloured powder produced by cooking, drying, and milling whole potatoes (Solanum tuberosum). Unlike potato starch, which is extracted from raw potato and contains only starch, potato flour retains the full nutritional profile of the cooked potato including protein, fibre, minerals, and natural sugars. It has a mild, earthy potato flavour and absorbs moisture readily, making it a useful thickener, binder, and partial flour substitute in gluten-free baking, soups, gravies, and dumplings. Nutritionally, potato flour is a high-carbohydrate ingredient (83.1 g/100g) with moderate protein (6.9 g/100g), very low fat (0.34 g/100g), and notable dietary fibre (5.9 g/100g). It is exceptionally rich in potassium (1,001 mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation and aligns with DASH dietary principles. Sodium is low at 55 mg/100g. Its high glycemic index (approximately 85) and high glycemic load mean it causes significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, making it unsuitable as a primary flour for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Potato flour is used globally in European, North American, and Asian cuisines as a functional ingredient rather than a standalone food. It is classified as a processed starchy tuber product.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between potato flour and potato starch?
Is potato flour good for high blood pressure?
How many calories in potato flour?
Is potato flour suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the glycemic index of potato flour?
Can I substitute potato flour for wheat flour?
Is potato flour high in potassium?
meal_context: thickening agent for soups and gravies
ingredient in gluten-free bread and pancakes

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05040009, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 20094 (Potato flour). GI value of 85 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for dehydrated potato products and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed reference. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a processed potato flour product.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.09 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.15 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 20094 (Potato flour, SR Legacy) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported by HPB is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this entry; USDA FoodData Central NDB 20094 reports some of these but values were not incorporated to avoid mixing source granularity without full cross-validation. Follow-up enrichment from USDA SR Legacy is recommended. 3. Glycemic load per serving is calculated for a 12 g (1 tablespoon) serving: GL = (85 × 9.26 g net carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 7.9. 4. Potato flour is a nightshade-derived product (Solanum tuberosum) and contains glycoalkaloids including solanine; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or nightshade sensitivity should exercise caution. 5. This record is not cuisine-specific (no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese classification applies); therefore G6 multilingual naming compliance is satisfied without mandatory local-language names beyond those provided for common retrieval utility.
